domains.description: Assesses whether AI is anchored to named business problems, owned outcomes, prioritised use cases, and executive decision rights
Assesses whether specific workflows have the right task profile, pain points, volume, stability, and review design for AI
domains.metric_name: Workflow & Use Case Readiness readiness score

domains.description: Assesses whether the structured data, documents, transcripts, and knowledge assets required for AI use cases are available, usable, and governed
Assesses whether core systems, integrations, insertion points, and technical ownership can support AI inside live workflows
Assesses whether processes are documented, controlled, and sufficiently standardised, and whether automation should be done before AI
Assesses whether approved data use, review controls, auditability, access, vendor risk, and oversight are strong enough for AI deployment
Assesses whether leaders, managers, and users understand AI, are ready to redesign work, and have adoption ownership and enablement in place
Assesses whether baselines, success criteria, pilot KPIs, financial value logic, and scale decision rules exist for AI use cases

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Has leadership clearly defined why the business wants to use AI?
questions.guidance: Confirm whether leadership has named specific business problems AI is meant to address, such as slow proposal turnaround, weak lead qualification, reporting delays, or service bottlenecks, rather than a broad desire to 'use AI'.
questions.evidence_examples: board papers; strategy deck; annual operating plan; transformation roadmap; executive interview notes
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: AI is discussed as a trend or brand signal with no defined business problem.
questions.score_3_guidance: One or two business problems are referenced, but the rationale is not documented or consistently understood.
questions.score_5_guidance: A documented AI thesis links named business problems, target workflows, and measurable outcomes.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are AI opportunities linked to measurable business priorities?
questions.guidance: Check whether proposed AI use cases map directly to business priorities such as revenue growth, margin protection, service speed, compliance control, or cost-to-serve reduction.
questions.evidence_examples: KPI tree; business scorecard; department goals; transformation business case; operating plan
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: AI ideas have no visible link to strategic or operational priorities.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some use cases map to priorities, though the links are incomplete or uneven across functions.
questions.score_5_guidance: All priority use cases are explicitly linked to owned KPIs, expected impact, and sponsoring leaders.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Has the business identified and ranked priority AI use cases?
questions.guidance: Look for a ranked shortlist that separates nice-to-have ideas from near-term pilot candidates using criteria such as value, feasibility, data readiness, risk, and time to value.
questions.evidence_examples: use case register; prioritization matrix; workshop outputs; pilot shortlist
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No AI use cases have been identified or documented.
questions.score_3_guidance: A shortlist exists, but prioritization is subjective or missing key criteria such as risk or feasibility.
questions.score_5_guidance: A maintained use case portfolio is ranked by value, feasibility, data readiness, risk, and ownership.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is there clear executive ownership for AI direction?
questions.guidance: Confirm whether one executive has authority to approve priorities, resolve trade-offs, and own AI direction across business and technology teams.
questions.evidence_examples: governance chart; steering committee terms; role descriptions; decision log
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No executive owner is accountable for AI direction.
questions.score_3_guidance: An owner is informally recognized, but authority or sponsorship is weak.
questions.score_5_guidance: An accountable executive sponsor is formally mandated, active, and integrated into governance.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Has budget or resource capacity been considered for AI initiatives?
questions.guidance: Assess whether the business has considered budget, people capacity, change effort, data work, and integration effort rather than only software subscription cost.
questions.evidence_examples: budget pack; resourcing plan; vendor estimates; project portfolio; hiring plan
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No budget, capacity, or delivery effort has been considered.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some resourcing has been discussed, but commitments are partial or optimistic.
questions.score_5_guidance: Funding, internal capacity, external support needs, and sequencing are clearly planned.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Has the business distinguished between automation opportunities and true AI opportunities?
questions.guidance: Test whether the business can distinguish between fixing the process, automating rules, and applying AI judgment, generation, retrieval, or prediction.
questions.evidence_examples: workflow redesign notes; automation inventory; use case screen; workshop outputs
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: All inefficiencies are being labelled as AI opportunities.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some teams distinguish automation from AI, but the logic is inconsistent.
questions.score_5_guidance: A clear decision method consistently separates process fixes, automation candidates, and true AI use cases.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is there a decision framework for evaluating new AI use cases?
questions.guidance: Review whether new ideas are assessed through a repeatable framework covering business value, feasibility, data dependency, integration complexity, risk, and ownership.
questions.evidence_examples: screening template; intake process; governance checklist; use case scorecard
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No consistent method exists to assess new AI requests.
questions.score_3_guidance: Basic evaluation criteria exist, but they are not consistently applied.
questions.score_5_guidance: A formal intake and screening process is consistently used and informs portfolio decisions.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is there a phased AI roadmap or pilot path aligned to business maturity?
questions.guidance: Check whether the roadmap sequences foundation work, automation cleanup, pilots, and scale in a way that matches current maturity rather than jumping straight to enterprise rollout.
questions.evidence_examples: AI roadmap; pilot plan; transformation plan; stage-gate model
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No roadmap or pilot path exists.
questions.score_3_guidance: A roadmap exists, but dependencies and maturity gates are only partly reflected.
questions.score_5_guidance: A staged roadmap clearly sequences foundations, pilots, scale decisions, and operating model changes.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Have target workflows for AI been clearly identified?
questions.guidance: Confirm that specific workflows have been named, bounded, and linked to departments, users, triggers, and outputs—for example proposal drafting, ticket triage, or meeting note capture.
questions.evidence_examples: process inventory; workflow maps; operations interviews; use case register
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No specific workflows have been identified.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some workflows are named, though boundaries, owners, or trigger points are still unclear.
questions.score_5_guidance: A documented workflow inventory links each AI candidate to owners, volume, users, and expected outcomes.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Do candidate workflows involve repetitive interpretation, drafting, classification, or decision support?
questions.guidance: Assess whether the workflow genuinely contains AI-suitable tasks such as summarizing calls, drafting first-pass content, classifying requests, searching knowledge, or supporting judgment-based routing.
questions.evidence_examples: task samples; SOPs; call notes; service queues; document examples
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: The shortlisted workflows do not contain meaningful AI-suitable tasks.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some tasks within the workflow fit AI, but the case is mixed.
questions.score_5_guidance: The workflow is rich in repetitive interpretation, drafting, retrieval, or decision-support tasks that strongly suit AI.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are target workflows high enough in volume or business importance to justify AI?
questions.guidance: Evaluate whether the workflow matters enough in terms of transaction volume, labor intensity, customer impact, cost of delay, or revenue consequence to justify intervention.
questions.evidence_examples: ticket volumes; turnaround data; SLA breaches; proposal counts; labor time analysis
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Selected workflows are too small, too rare, or too low-value to justify AI.
questions.score_3_guidance: The workflow has moderate value or volume, but the commercial case is not yet strong.
questions.score_5_guidance: The workflow is a high-impact candidate with quantified volume, pain, and value potential.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are the pain points within target workflows clearly understood?
questions.guidance: Check whether the current pain is defined precisely—such as rework, slow turnaround, missed follow-up, inconsistent answers, backlog growth, or supervisor dependency.
questions.evidence_examples: root-cause analysis; issue logs; escalation themes; workshop notes; quality reviews
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Pain points are vague or assumed, with no real understanding of the problem.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some pain points are known, though root causes and impact are not consistently mapped.
questions.score_5_guidance: Pain points are mapped to workflow stages, root causes, and measurable business impact.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are target workflows documented well enough to redesign?
questions.guidance: Review whether the workflow is documented at a level that shows actors, sequence, systems used, decisions, handoffs, inputs, outputs, and exception paths.
questions.evidence_examples: process map; SOP; swimlane; desk procedure; workflow screenshot pack
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: The workflow is undocumented or only known informally.
questions.score_3_guidance: Core steps are documented, but handoffs, decisions, or exceptions are incomplete.
questions.score_5_guidance: An end-to-end workflow view exists with roles, systems, handoffs, decisions, inputs, outputs, and exceptions.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are target workflows stable and repeatable rather than chaotic?
questions.guidance: Assess whether the workflow follows a stable pattern often enough for AI to perform consistently, even if some exceptions exist.
questions.evidence_examples: variation analysis; supervisor interviews; case samples; quality audits
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: The workflow changes case by case and is too chaotic for consistent AI use.
questions.score_3_guidance: The workflow has a repeatable core, but variation is still material.
questions.score_5_guidance: The workflow has a strong repeatable pattern and a well-understood exception profile.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Has the business identified where basic automation is enough and AI is unnecessary?
questions.guidance: Look for evidence that the business has screened out tasks that should be handled through forms, rules, routing, templates, or workflow automation instead of AI.
questions.evidence_examples: automation backlog; redesign workshop notes; solution options analysis
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: The business is trying to use AI for tasks that are basic automation or process design issues.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some automation-only opportunities are recognized, but the distinction is incomplete.
questions.score_5_guidance: The solution selection process consistently reserves AI for tasks that require interpretation, generation, retrieval, or prediction.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are there clear human review points in workflows where AI may be introduced?
questions.guidance: Confirm where human review sits in the workflow: who checks the output, at what stage, under what risk conditions, and what happens when the AI result is wrong or uncertain.
questions.evidence_examples: approval matrix; SOP; QA checklist; escalation flow
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No human review points have been defined.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some review points exist, but they are inconsistent or incomplete.
questions.score_5_guidance: Clear human-in-the-loop controls specify owners, triggers, escalation paths, and override rules.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is the data required for priority AI use cases already captured?
questions.guidance: Check whether the data inputs needed for the chosen use cases already exist in usable form, including structured fields, documents, tickets, call transcripts, or knowledge assets.
questions.evidence_examples: data inventory; source system extracts; document repositories; transcript sources
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: The core data needed for the priority use cases is not being captured.
questions.score_3_guidance: Most core inputs exist, though there are notable gaps or workarounds.
questions.score_5_guidance: The full set of core structured and unstructured inputs is already captured for priority use cases.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is business data stored in accessible systems rather than scattered informally?
questions.guidance: Assess whether data lives in systems that can be queried, exported, or connected, rather than in personal inboxes, local files, or uncontrolled shared folders.
questions.evidence_examples: system screenshots; shared drive structure; inbox dependency review; data access map
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Important data is mostly trapped in personal or informal locations.
questions.score_3_guidance: There is a mix of system-based storage and informal storage that creates friction.
questions.score_5_guidance: Core business data is centrally stored, accessible, and operationally retrievable.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are key data fields standardized across systems or teams?
questions.guidance: Review whether the same business fields—such as customer status, lead source, ticket category, product code, or job stage—mean the same thing across teams and systems.
questions.evidence_examples: data dictionary; field mapping; CRM configuration; reporting definitions
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Critical fields are inconsistent or undefined across teams and systems.
questions.score_3_guidance: Core fields are partly standardized, though important inconsistencies remain.
questions.score_5_guidance: Key fields are standardized, governed, and consistently used across systems and teams.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is data quality sufficient for reliable AI outputs?
questions.guidance: Assess whether duplicates, missing values, stale records, misclassification, and poor document hygiene would materially degrade AI output quality.
questions.evidence_examples: data quality audit; exception report; duplicate analysis; sample record review
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Data quality is poor enough to make AI outputs unreliable.
questions.score_3_guidance: Quality is acceptable in parts, but important gaps would still affect pilots.
questions.score_5_guidance: Data quality is actively controlled and strong enough to support reliable AI outputs.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is there enough historical data depth for the intended use cases?
questions.guidance: Check whether enough historical depth exists to support the intended use case, especially for trend analysis, forecasting, retrieval quality, pattern detection, or performance comparison.
questions.evidence_examples: historic exports; archive depth review; reporting history; retention policy
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: There is not enough history to support the intended use cases.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some history exists, but depth is uneven or insufficient for certain use cases.
questions.score_5_guidance: Strong historical depth exists across the main datasets needed for pilot and scale use cases.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are unstructured knowledge sources organized and searchable?
questions.guidance: Review whether policies, SOPs, FAQs, product information, case notes, and other unstructured content are organised, current, and searchable enough for retrieval-based AI.
questions.evidence_examples: knowledge base; SharePoint structure; SOP library; FAQ repository; document taxonomy
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Unstructured knowledge is disorganized, outdated, or largely unusable.
questions.score_3_guidance: Key content is partly organized, though gaps in structure or freshness remain.
questions.score_5_guidance: Knowledge assets are curated, current, tagged or structured, and ready for retrieval-based AI.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are data owners defined for critical datasets?
questions.guidance: Confirm whether named owners are responsible for data quality, definitions, access, and remediation for the datasets that matter to the AI use cases.
questions.evidence_examples: RACI matrix; data owner register; role descriptions; stewardship model
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No one is clearly accountable for critical datasets.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some important datasets have owners, but accountability is incomplete.
questions.score_5_guidance: All priority datasets have clear owners responsible for quality, access, and definition control.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is there a reliable source of truth for customer, operational, or financial data?
questions.guidance: Assess whether the business has a trusted primary source for customer, operational, and financial facts rather than competing spreadsheets or conflicting reports.
questions.evidence_examples: source-of-truth map; BI model; finance reporting logic; master data register
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: There is no trusted source of truth for critical business data.
questions.score_3_guidance: A partial source-of-truth model exists, but some important areas still conflict.
questions.score_5_guidance: The organisation uses clearly defined and widely accepted sources of truth across key domains.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are the core business systems documented and understood?
questions.guidance: Confirm whether the business can list its core systems, what each one does, which workflows depend on them, who administers them, and where major data lives.
questions.evidence_examples: application inventory; architecture diagram; admin list; workflow-system map
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: The core system landscape is not documented or understood.
questions.score_3_guidance: Most major systems are known, but workflow role, ownership, or dependencies are incomplete.
questions.score_5_guidance: A maintained system inventory shows purpose, ownership, data role, and workflow dependencies.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Do key systems support integration methods needed for AI deployment?
questions.guidance: Assess whether the relevant systems expose APIs, webhooks, integration connectors, secure exports, or workflow tooling needed to move data and actions into or out of AI services.
questions.evidence_examples: vendor docs; API references; middleware catalogue; admin settings; integration review
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Key systems offer no practical integration method for the target use cases.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some required systems support integration, but important gaps remain.
questions.score_5_guidance: The main systems supporting priority use cases have strong and practical integration options.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Can AI outputs be inserted back into real workflows or systems?
questions.guidance: Check whether AI outputs can land where the user already works—such as CRM notes, ticket queues, proposal templates, inbox workflows, or approval tools—rather than living in a disconnected side app.
questions.evidence_examples: workflow mockups; system fields; UI screenshots; process redesign notes
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: AI outputs would remain outside the live workflow and require manual re-entry.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some outputs could be inserted into workflow tools, but coverage is incomplete.
questions.score_5_guidance: AI outputs can be embedded directly into the system steps where users already act.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are teams overly dependent on manual exports, imports, or copy-paste between systems?
questions.guidance: Measure how much the current process depends on CSV exports, spreadsheet updates, copy-paste, email forwarding, or manual rekeying between systems.
questions.evidence_examples: handoff analysis; user interviews; screen recordings; ops walkthroughs
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: The workflow is heavily dependent on manual transfers between systems.
questions.score_3_guidance: There is a mix of manual and automated movement that still creates friction.
questions.score_5_guidance: Data and task movement across systems is largely automated or system-native.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are system handoffs and dependencies visible enough to support AI design?
questions.guidance: Review whether upstream and downstream dependencies are visible enough to show what triggers the workflow, where data enters, what breaks, and which teams or systems are affected by change.
questions.evidence_examples: dependency map; service blueprint; integration flow; support incident themes
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: System handoffs and dependencies are largely invisible.
questions.score_3_guidance: The main dependencies are known, but not all failure points or handoffs are mapped.
questions.score_5_guidance: An end-to-end dependency view exists, including triggers, handoffs, failure points, and affected owners.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is there a practical technical path to implement the top AI use cases?
questions.guidance: Assess whether there is a realistic implementation path covering data flow, security, user touchpoint, integration approach, and delivery ownership for the top use cases.
questions.evidence_examples: solution outline; architecture option paper; pilot design; technical discovery notes
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No practical implementation path has been identified.
questions.score_3_guidance: A workable path is visible, but some dependencies or constraints remain unclear.
questions.score_5_guidance: A well-defined technical path exists for priority pilots, with manageable dependencies and constraints.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is there technical ownership for integrations, automations, or AI deployment?
questions.guidance: Confirm whether technical ownership is assigned across solution design, integration build, security review, support, and post-launch maintenance.
questions.evidence_examples: delivery RACI; IT operating model; project plan; support ownership matrix
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No one clearly owns technical delivery or support for AI-related work.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some technical ownership exists, but gaps remain across build, security, or support.
questions.score_5_guidance: Named technical owners cover design, build, security, support, and change control.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are current systems robust enough to support pilot deployment without major disruption?
questions.guidance: Evaluate whether current systems can absorb a pilot without causing instability, major downtime risk, or unacceptable workarounds for users.
questions.evidence_examples: incident trends; platform health reports; release constraints; admin interviews
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Current systems are too fragile or overloaded to support a pilot safely.
questions.score_3_guidance: A pilot is possible, but reliability or support risk needs close management.
questions.score_5_guidance: The environment is stable, supported, and operationally ready for pilot activity.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are target processes documented and repeatable?
questions.guidance: Review whether the target process is formally described and can be executed the same way by different people using the same inputs, outputs, and steps.
questions.evidence_examples: SOP; desk instructions; playbook; process map; onboarding guide
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: The process is undocumented and varies by person.
questions.score_3_guidance: Core steps are documented, though consistency still depends on individual practice.
questions.score_5_guidance: The process is well-documented, stable, and repeatable across users and locations.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are teams executing the same process consistently?
questions.guidance: Assess whether teams follow the same business rules, thresholds, escalation paths, and completion standards when executing the process.
questions.evidence_examples: quality reviews; supervisor observations; calibration notes; audit samples
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Different people or teams run the process in materially different ways.
questions.score_3_guidance: The process is consistent in core areas, but variation still appears in important cases.
questions.score_5_guidance: Execution is highly consistent, calibrated, and monitored across the process owners.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are obvious rule-based steps already automated or at least identified?
questions.guidance: Look for rule-based steps that should already be solved with templates, routing rules, validations, or workflow automation before AI is added.
questions.evidence_examples: automation inventory; BPM backlog; workflow redesign notes; system rules review
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Obvious automation candidates have not been identified and AI is being considered too early.
questions.score_3_guidance: Basic automation opportunities have been identified, though not consistently addressed.
questions.score_5_guidance: Rule-based tasks are largely automated or intentionally excluded before AI is introduced.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are process inputs and outputs clearly defined?
questions.guidance: Confirm whether the process has defined inputs, expected outputs, acceptance criteria, and data handoff points so that AI has a clear operating frame.
questions.evidence_examples: form design; SOP; template pack; control checklist
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Inputs and outputs are unclear or inconsistent.
questions.score_3_guidance: Key inputs and outputs are partly defined, though some ambiguity remains.
questions.score_5_guidance: Inputs, outputs, acceptance criteria, and handoffs are clearly defined and controlled.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are exception paths understood and manageable?
questions.guidance: Assess whether exception scenarios are known, categorized, and handled through clear escalation or override logic instead of ad hoc intervention.
questions.evidence_examples: exception log; escalation matrix; supervisor notes; quality issue tracker
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Exception handling is chaotic and largely unmanaged.
questions.score_3_guidance: Common exception types are known, but coverage or escalation rules are incomplete.
questions.score_5_guidance: Exception categories, thresholds, owners, and escalation paths are clearly defined.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Do target processes rely heavily on tribal knowledge?
questions.guidance: Check whether success depends on undocumented know-how held by a few experienced staff, such as how to interpret requests, route cases, or judge completeness.
questions.evidence_examples: expert interviews; onboarding material; shadowing notes; SOP gaps
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: The process depends heavily on tribal knowledge with little documentation.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some undocumented know-how remains, though parts have been captured.
questions.score_5_guidance: The process has low dependency on tribal knowledge because operating logic is clearly documented.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Has the business stabilized broken processes before trying to apply AI?
questions.guidance: Review whether the business has already fixed major process instability—such as duplicate handoffs, missing approvals, unclear ownership, or broken intake—before positioning AI as the answer.
questions.evidence_examples: continuous improvement log; redesign plan; root-cause actions; ops review
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Broken or unstable processes are being targeted for AI without prior stabilisation.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some stabilisation work has happened, but significant weaknesses are still present.
questions.score_5_guidance: The business has deliberately stabilised weak processes before considering AI augmentation.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Can target processes support human oversight without breaking workflow speed?
questions.guidance: Assess whether human oversight can be inserted into the process without destroying cycle time, creating new queues, or overloading managers.
questions.evidence_examples: timing analysis; QA design; reviewer capacity plan; approval SLA
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Human review would make the process too slow or operationally unworkable.
questions.score_3_guidance: A workable oversight model exists, but it may still create bottlenecks in some cases.
questions.score_5_guidance: Oversight fits cleanly into workflow timing, capacity, and quality control requirements.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are there rules for what data can and cannot be used with AI tools?
questions.guidance: Confirm whether staff know what company, customer, employee, financial, or regulated data may be used with approved AI tools and what is prohibited.
questions.evidence_examples: AI acceptable-use policy; data handling standard; security awareness material; tool policy
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No rules exist on what data may be used with AI tools.
questions.score_3_guidance: Basic rules exist, though important edge cases or data classes are not covered.
questions.score_5_guidance: A formal policy defines allowed use, prohibited use, approved tools, and handling rules by data type.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are sensitive data categories identified and protected in AI-related workflows?
questions.guidance: Assess whether sensitive data categories are formally identified and protected in workflows involving prompts, uploads, retrieval, storage, or model outputs.
questions.evidence_examples: data classification policy; privacy controls; masking rules; access matrix
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Sensitive data categories are not identified or protected for AI use.
questions.score_3_guidance: Important sensitive categories are known, though protection measures are incomplete.
questions.score_5_guidance: Sensitive data classes are clearly classified with strong protection, restriction, and monitoring controls.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are human approval requirements defined for higher-risk AI outputs?
questions.guidance: Check whether higher-risk outputs—such as customer communications, financial commentary, legal content, HR decisions, or policy advice—require defined human approval before use.
questions.evidence_examples: approval matrix; risk policy; SOP; quality control workflow
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No human approval rules exist for higher-risk AI outputs.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some high-risk cases require review, but the rules are not complete or consistent.
questions.score_5_guidance: Approval rules are formally defined by output type, risk level, owner, and escalation path.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Can AI-generated outputs be traced, reviewed, or audited?
questions.guidance: Review whether the business can trace which prompts, data sources, model outputs, reviewers, and final actions were involved when AI is used in a workflow.
questions.evidence_examples: system logs; audit trail; version history; review records; platform settings
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: AI activity cannot be traced or audited in a meaningful way.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some traceability exists, but it is incomplete across sources, outputs, or approvals.
questions.score_5_guidance: A strong audit trail covers prompts, inputs, outputs, review actions, and final decisions.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are access controls in place for data and systems used by AI workflows?
questions.guidance: Assess whether least-privilege access, role separation, and secure permissions are already in place for the systems and datasets involved in AI workflows.
questions.evidence_examples: role matrix; system permissions; IAM review; admin settings
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Access to relevant systems and data is poorly controlled.
questions.score_3_guidance: Controls are acceptable in parts, though some gaps remain in critical areas.
questions.score_5_guidance: Role-based access and security controls are robust, monitored, and appropriate for AI deployment.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is there a process to assess vendor, model, or platform risk?
questions.guidance: Check whether external AI vendors, models, or platforms are screened for security, privacy, data retention, contractual, and operational risk before use.
questions.evidence_examples: vendor checklist; procurement review; security questionnaire; legal review
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: There is no process to assess AI vendors, models, or platforms.
questions.score_3_guidance: A basic review process exists, but it is incomplete or inconsistently applied.
questions.score_5_guidance: A formal cross-functional process reviews vendor, model, privacy, security, and operational risk before approval.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are legal, compliance, or regulatory obligations understood for target use cases?
questions.guidance: Confirm whether legal, regulatory, contractual, or industry-specific obligations have been identified for the selected use cases, especially where customer data, employment decisions, finance, or communications are involved.
questions.evidence_examples: legal memo; compliance review; contract obligations; regulatory checklist
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Relevant legal or compliance obligations have not been assessed.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some obligations are understood, though not all priority use cases have been reviewed.
questions.score_5_guidance: A clear legal and compliance position exists for each priority use case and is reflected in design controls.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is there a defined governance owner or forum for AI risk oversight?
questions.guidance: Assess whether there is a named owner or forum that reviews AI risk, policy exceptions, pilot approvals, incidents, and ongoing control performance.
questions.evidence_examples: governance committee charter; risk forum notes; RACI; policy owner register
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No governance owner or forum exists for AI risk oversight.
questions.score_3_guidance: A partial owner or forum exists, but cadence or authority is weak.
questions.score_5_guidance: An active governance structure reviews approvals, incidents, policy adherence, and control effectiveness on a defined cadence.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Do leaders understand the realistic strengths and limits of AI?
questions.guidance: Check whether leaders understand where AI helps, where it fails, what needs human oversight, and why process/data readiness matters before rollout.
questions.evidence_examples: leadership interviews; workshop outputs; executive briefing notes; strategy discussions
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Leaders largely view AI through hype, fear, or vendor claims rather than practical operating reality.
questions.score_3_guidance: Leaders have a reasonable high-level view, but lack depth on delivery and operating implications.
questions.score_5_guidance: Leaders show practical understanding of AI capabilities, failure modes, governance needs, and adoption requirements.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are managers prepared to redesign workflows around AI support?
questions.guidance: Assess whether managers are ready to redesign roles, workflows, controls, and performance expectations when AI is introduced into day-to-day operations.
questions.evidence_examples: manager interviews; operating model workshop; department plans; pilot readiness notes
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Managers are not prepared to change how work is organised around AI.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some managers are prepared, but readiness is uneven across pilot areas.
questions.score_5_guidance: Managers are actively engaged in redesigning workflows, roles, and oversight around AI support.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are target users likely to trust and use AI outputs appropriately?
questions.guidance: Review whether the intended users trust AI enough to use it, while also understanding when to challenge, correct, or escalate outputs.
questions.evidence_examples: user interviews; readiness survey; pilot feedback; workshop notes
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Users are likely to reject or misuse AI outputs due to very low trust or understanding.
questions.score_3_guidance: User readiness is mixed, with openness in some areas but skepticism or confusion in others.
questions.score_5_guidance: Users show healthy trust, know when to review outputs, and are likely to use AI appropriately in workflow.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is resistance to workflow change likely to block adoption?
questions.guidance: Assess whether workflow change is likely to be blocked by fear of job impact, quality concerns, control concerns, manager resistance, or past change fatigue.
questions.evidence_examples: stakeholder map; interview themes; change risk log; pulse survey
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Resistance is likely to materially block rollout in the target areas.
questions.score_3_guidance: There is some resistance, but it appears manageable with focused change work.
questions.score_5_guidance: Change resistance is low and the organisation shows good readiness to adapt operating practices.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are training plans in place for teams affected by AI changes?
questions.guidance: Check whether training is planned for affected users, reviewers, managers, and support teams, covering both tool usage and new workflow responsibilities.
questions.evidence_examples: training plan; enablement deck; role-based curriculum; onboarding plan
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No training plan exists for affected teams.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some training is planned, though scope or audience coverage is incomplete.
questions.score_5_guidance: Role-based training is fully planned across users, reviewers, managers, and support teams.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is there a clear owner for AI adoption, not just technology delivery?
questions.guidance: Confirm whether someone owns adoption outcomes such as usage, compliance with the new process, feedback capture, and correction of low uptake—not just technical launch.
questions.evidence_examples: project governance; adoption KPI owner; RACI; change plan
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No one owns adoption after the tool is introduced.
questions.score_3_guidance: Partial adoption ownership exists, but responsibilities are not complete.
questions.score_5_guidance: A named owner is accountable for adoption metrics, feedback loops, and corrective action.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Do teams understand how human oversight will work in practice?
questions.guidance: Assess whether teams understand what they are expected to review, when they must override AI, what quality standard applies, and who is accountable for final decisions.
questions.evidence_examples: workflow guide; SOP; QA framework; reviewer training notes
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Teams do not understand how oversight should work in practice.
questions.score_3_guidance: Basic oversight roles are defined, but clarity is still uneven across teams.
questions.score_5_guidance: Oversight responsibilities, review triggers, escalation, and accountability are clearly understood across teams.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is cross-functional collaboration strong enough to support AI implementation?
questions.guidance: Check whether business, operations, data, risk, and technology teams can work together quickly enough to design, approve, deploy, and improve AI use cases.
questions.evidence_examples: governance cadence; delivery squad structure; issue log; operating rhythm
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Cross-functional collaboration is too weak or siloed to support implementation.
questions.score_3_guidance: Collaboration works in some areas, but coordination is still inconsistent.
questions.score_5_guidance: Cross-functional collaboration is strong, with clear forums, decision paths, and delivery rhythm.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are baseline metrics available for target workflows?
questions.guidance: Confirm whether current baseline measures exist for the selected workflows, such as turnaround time, backlog, conversion, error rate, QA score, handle time, or cost per task.
questions.evidence_examples: dashboard; workflow report; SLA report; finance analysis; quality scorecard
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No baseline performance data exists for the target workflows.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some useful baseline metrics exist, but not enough for strong comparison.
questions.score_5_guidance: A strong baseline exists across the key performance measures needed to assess pilot impact.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Has success been clearly defined for each priority AI use case?
questions.guidance: Review whether success is defined per use case in concrete terms, such as reducing turnaround time by a target percentage, improving win rate, lowering rework, or increasing knowledge retrieval accuracy.
questions.evidence_examples: pilot charter; business case; KPI definition sheet; sponsor approval
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Success has not been defined for the priority use cases.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some measurable success criteria exist, but not for every main use case.
questions.score_5_guidance: Each priority use case has explicit, measurable, approved success criteria.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Can impact be measured in operational or commercial terms?
questions.guidance: Check whether the business can translate AI impact into operational and commercial terms such as labor saved, backlog reduced, SLA improvement, conversion uplift, margin gain, or risk reduction.
questions.evidence_examples: value model; finance case; operations KPI map; service value analysis
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: The business cannot express AI impact in measurable business terms.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some impact pathways are defined, though the model is incomplete.
questions.score_5_guidance: Impact can be measured in clear business terms and tied to named value drivers.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are pilot KPIs defined before implementation starts?
questions.guidance: Assess whether pilot KPIs are agreed before launch, including adoption, quality, efficiency, control, and business outcome measures where relevant.
questions.evidence_examples: pilot scorecard; launch checklist; governance pack; test plan
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: Pilot KPIs are not defined before implementation.
questions.score_3_guidance: A partial KPI set exists, though gaps remain in quality, control, or business outcome measures.
questions.score_5_guidance: Pilots have a fully defined pre-launch KPI scorecard covering outcome, efficiency, quality, control, and adoption.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Is there a method to compare pre-AI and post-AI performance?
questions.guidance: Confirm whether there is a disciplined method to compare performance before and after AI, including timing, control group or baseline choice, and review cadence.
questions.evidence_examples: evaluation method; baseline plan; PMO scorecard; experiment design
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: There is no method to compare pre-AI and post-AI performance.
questions.score_3_guidance: A basic comparison method exists, but it lacks rigor or consistency.
questions.score_5_guidance: A formal evaluation approach defines baseline, review cadence, comparison method, and evidence standards.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are scaling thresholds defined for successful pilots?
questions.guidance: Check whether the business has defined the threshold for scaling, redesigning, pausing, or stopping a pilot based on evidence rather than enthusiasm.
questions.evidence_examples: stage-gate criteria; governance pack; pilot decision framework
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No scale or stop criteria exist for successful or unsuccessful pilots.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some decision criteria exist, but thresholds or governance triggers are incomplete.
questions.score_5_guidance: Formal scale, redesign, and stop thresholds are defined and tied to governance decisions.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Are owners accountable for tracking AI outcomes after deployment?
questions.guidance: Confirm whether named owners are responsible for tracking outcomes after launch, maintaining the KPI view, and escalating underperformance or control failures.
questions.evidence_examples: owner register; KPI governance; PMO cadence; business review pack
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No one is accountable for tracking AI outcomes after launch.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some accountability exists, but ownership is fragmented or incomplete.
questions.score_5_guidance: Named owners are accountable for measurement, reporting, and corrective action after deployment.

questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether AI can be adopted safely, practically, and against real business priorities.
questions.question_text: Can the business estimate financial value for the top AI use cases?
questions.guidance: Assess whether the business can estimate financial value using assumptions grounded in throughput, labor cost, revenue conversion, service capacity, error reduction, or risk avoidance.
questions.evidence_examples: financial model; ROI estimate; sponsor business case; operations economics
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Weak readiness means AI deployment will be slower, riskier, or poorly aligned to business value.
questions.score_1_guidance: No financial value estimate exists for the priority AI use cases.
questions.score_3_guidance: A basic financial estimate exists, but key assumptions are weak or incomplete.
questions.score_5_guidance: Finance-backed value estimates exist for priority use cases and are tied to explicit operating assumptions.

findings.finding_title: AI intent is trend-led rather than business-led
findings.finding_text: Leadership interest in AI is present, but the business has not yet translated that interest into a defined commercial thesis, an owned problem statement, or a ranked set of use cases.
findings.business_impact: Investment decisions are likely to be reactive, and resources may be spent on tools before priority business problems are agreed.

findings.finding_title: AI direction exists but portfolio discipline is still weak
findings.finding_text: The business has started to define where AI may help, but ownership, ranking logic, and sequencing are not yet strong enough to govern investment confidently.
findings.business_impact: Promising ideas may compete for attention without a clear basis for selecting the best pilots first.

findings.finding_title: AI strategy is commercially grounded and governable
findings.finding_text: The business has articulated why AI matters, which use cases come first, who owns decisions, and how priorities link back to business outcomes.
findings.business_impact: The organisation is in a stronger position to approve pilots based on value, readiness, and execution discipline rather than hype.

findings.finding_title: Candidate workflows are not yet ready for credible AI selection
findings.finding_text: The business has not yet narrowed AI down to clearly bounded workflows with defined pain points, stable patterns, and operational review design.
findings.business_impact: AI may be pointed at the wrong work, or introduced into processes that still need process mapping and stabilisation first.

findings.finding_title: Use case candidates are visible but workflow design still needs tightening
findings.finding_text: Several workflows appear promising for AI, but workflow boundaries, root pain points, or human-in-the-loop controls are not yet complete enough for confident pilot design.
findings.business_impact: Pilot quality and adoption may suffer because the workflow operating model is not fully locked down.

findings.finding_title: High-fit workflows have been identified with practical pilot potential
findings.finding_text: The business has isolated workflows with clear pain, repeatable patterns, meaningful volume, and tasks that genuinely suit AI support such as drafting, classification, retrieval, or summarisation.
findings.business_impact: Pilot selection can focus on workflows where AI has a stronger chance of improving speed, quality, or consistency.

findings.finding_title: Priority use cases are constrained by weak data foundations
findings.finding_text: The required data for the shortlisted AI use cases is fragmented, inconsistently defined, poor in quality, weakly owned, or difficult to retrieve in a controlled way.
findings.business_impact: AI outputs will be unreliable, difficult to trust, or expensive to operationalise until the data foundation is improved.

findings.finding_title: Data is usable in parts, but not yet consistently deployment-ready
findings.finding_text: The business has enough data to explore selected use cases, but important gaps remain in ownership, structure, access, history, or knowledge organisation.
findings.business_impact: AI pilots may work in controlled scope, but scaling will be limited unless the supporting data foundation is tightened.

findings.finding_title: Data foundations are sufficiently strong for priority AI use cases
findings.finding_text: The main structured and unstructured inputs needed for the chosen use cases exist, are accessible, and are governed well enough to support controlled deployment.
findings.business_impact: The business can move toward pilot design with greater confidence in output quality, retrieval quality, and operational trust.

findings.finding_title: System fragmentation will restrict operational AI value
findings.finding_text: The application landscape is not yet cleanly documented or connected enough to move AI outputs into the live workflow without manual workarounds, fragile handoffs, or support risk.
findings.business_impact: Even well-chosen use cases may fail to create business value because outputs remain disconnected from the systems where teams actually work.

findings.finding_title: There is a technical path, but workflow embedding is not yet strong enough
findings.finding_text: Relevant systems can support parts of the AI solution, but manual transfers, incomplete dependency mapping, or limited insertion points still weaken the path from pilot to production.
findings.business_impact: A pilot may run, but operational adoption and scale will be harder unless integration design is strengthened.

findings.finding_title: The technology environment can support embedded AI delivery
findings.finding_text: The core systems, integration methods, ownership, and workflow insertion points are sufficiently clear to place AI outputs inside real operational processes.
findings.business_impact: The business can pursue pilots that work inside existing systems rather than adding another disconnected tool.

findings.finding_title: Process discipline is not yet strong enough for AI layering
findings.finding_text: Target workflows remain unstable, inconsistently executed, dependent on tribal knowledge, or crowded with unaddressed rule-based tasks that should be fixed before AI is introduced.
findings.business_impact: AI would add complexity to weak processes and is likely to amplify inconsistency instead of reducing it.

findings.finding_title: The process base is improving, but selected workflows still need control tightening
findings.finding_text: Core workflows have some repeatability, but variation, unclear exceptions, partial SOP coverage, or weak automation discipline still reduce AI readiness.
findings.business_impact: AI may be viable in limited areas, but process maturity gaps will still constrain quality, scalability, and reviewer burden.

findings.finding_title: Processes are mature enough to support controlled AI augmentation
findings.finding_text: The priority workflows are sufficiently documented, repeatable, and operationally controlled to support AI assistance without destabilising the process.
findings.business_impact: The business can introduce AI into workflows where process variation and oversight burden are less likely to undermine results.

findings.finding_title: AI control requirements are not yet strong enough for safe deployment
findings.finding_text: Rules for approved data use, sensitive data handling, human approval, traceability, vendor review, and governance ownership are incomplete or weak for the intended AI scenarios.
findings.business_impact: The organisation is exposed to avoidable privacy, security, compliance, and accountability risk if deployment moves ahead too quickly.

findings.finding_title: Basic AI controls exist, but operating governance is still incomplete
findings.finding_text: The business has started to define acceptable use and control expectations, but auditability, risk ownership, legal interpretation, or approval logic still need strengthening for live deployment.
findings.business_impact: Low-risk pilots may be possible, but governance gaps increase exposure as use cases become more material or externally facing.

findings.finding_title: Governance foundations can support responsible AI use
findings.finding_text: The business has workable policy, review, audit, access, and oversight structures for the selected AI scenarios.
findings.business_impact: AI can be introduced with clearer control boundaries, accountability, and decision rights.

findings.finding_title: Adoption risk is likely to undermine implementation
findings.finding_text: Leadership, managers, or users do not yet show enough practical understanding, trust, ownership, or change readiness to absorb AI into the way work is actually done.
findings.business_impact: Even a technically sound deployment could underperform because teams may resist it, misuse it, or fail to change the workflow around it.

findings.finding_title: Readiness to adopt AI is visible but still uneven
findings.finding_text: There is interest and some momentum for AI, but training, manager readiness, oversight clarity, or ownership of adoption still need to mature.
findings.business_impact: Pilots may gain traction, but sustained usage and operating change are not yet guaranteed.

findings.finding_title: The business has the people conditions needed for adoption
findings.finding_text: Leaders understand the practical role of AI, managers are ready to adapt workflows, users are reasonably prepared, and adoption ownership is visible.
findings.business_impact: The organisation is better placed to move from pilot launch to actual usage and workflow change.

findings.finding_title: The business cannot yet prove AI value with confidence
findings.finding_text: Baseline measures, success definitions, pilot scorecards, financial value logic, or post-launch accountability are not strong enough to evaluate AI as an investment.
findings.business_impact: AI may be funded as an experiment, but the business will struggle to know whether to scale, redesign, or stop.

findings.finding_title: Measurement logic exists, but not yet at decision-grade strength
findings.finding_text: Some value measures and pilot KPIs are visible, but comparisons, scale thresholds, or financial assumptions still need to be tightened.
findings.business_impact: AI results may look promising, but scaling decisions will still rely partly on judgment rather than evidence.

findings.finding_title: AI value can be measured, governed, and used for scale decisions
findings.finding_text: The business has clear baselines, success metrics, comparison logic, and financial framing for the priority use cases.
findings.business_impact: Leadership can make stronger go, pause, redesign, or scale decisions based on evidence rather than enthusiasm.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Define a business-led AI thesis before discussing tools
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Run an executive alignment exercise that names the business problems AI should address, the outcomes it should move, the workflows that matter first, and the sponsor accountable for decisions.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Formalise the AI portfolio, ranking logic, and ownership model
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Create a standing use case intake and prioritisation method covering value, feasibility, data dependency, risk, and ownership so the best pilot candidates rise to the top consistently.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Translate strategy into a governed pilot portfolio
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Convert the approved AI strategy into a small pilot portfolio with sequencing, named sponsors, stage gates, and business-value expectations for each use case.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Map the real workflows before selecting AI interventions
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Document the candidate workflows end to end, isolate the actual failure points, and distinguish process redesign and automation needs from true AI opportunities.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Tighten workflow design for shortlisted AI use cases
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Strengthen workflow boundaries, pain-point definition, review points, and suitability logic so the shortlisted use cases are pilot-ready rather than conceptually promising only.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Advance the best-fit workflows into pilot design
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Take the most stable, high-value workflows and build pilot-ready operating designs including triggers, user touchpoints, review steps, and expected outcomes.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Build the minimum viable data foundation for the selected pilots
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Identify the exact data inputs each pilot needs, clean critical fields, organise supporting knowledge, and assign ownership so pilot outputs are grounded in usable data.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Improve data consistency, access, and ownership around priority use cases
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Standardise key fields, tighten data quality controls, improve access to the supporting data and knowledge assets, and close the biggest ownership gaps.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Package approved data and knowledge inputs for controlled pilot use
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Curate the structured datasets and knowledge assets that will feed the pilots, including freshness rules, ownership, and controls for ongoing maintenance.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Define a realistic embed-and-integrate path before deployment
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Map where data enters, where AI outputs should land, which systems need to connect, and which manual handoffs must be removed for the selected workflows to work in practice.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Strengthen workflow embedding and orchestration for pilots
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Design cleaner insertion points, reduce manual transfers, and clarify dependency and support ownership so pilot outputs can sit inside the live workflow reliably.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Use the existing technology base to embed AI into operational tools
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Implement the pilots inside the systems of work already used by teams, rather than launching a disconnected side workflow.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Stabilise the process and automate rules before adding AI
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Resolve core process instability, close SOP gaps, handle obvious automation candidates, and reduce dependence on tribal workarounds before AI is introduced.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Tighten process control in the selected workflows
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Clarify business rules, exception handling, handoffs, and oversight design so AI operates inside a cleaner and more consistent process.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Introduce AI first where process maturity is already strongest
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Start with the most repeatable workflows and use those pilots to prove value before expanding into more variable or exception-heavy processes.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Put AI operating controls in place before any material rollout
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Define approved data-use rules, sensitive-data restrictions, review requirements, vendor checks, and governance ownership for the selected AI scenarios.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Convert policy into working control mechanisms for pilots
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Build the review matrix, audit trail expectations, access controls, and risk review process needed to govern real pilot workflows rather than principle only.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Embed governance into pilot delivery and scale decisions
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Use the existing control foundation to govern approvals, exceptions, incidents, and evidence-based scale decisions across the AI portfolio.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Build adoption readiness before launch
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Strengthen leadership understanding, assign adoption ownership, address resistance drivers, and prepare the affected teams for how work and oversight will change.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Formalise training, manager enablement, and change support
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Create role-based enablement for managers, users, reviewers, and support teams so pilot adoption is actively managed rather than assumed.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Use the existing change capacity to scale adoption deliberately
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Expand from early pilots through manager alignment, champions, feedback loops, and operating-model reinforcement rather than one-off communications.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Define value, baseline, and success criteria before further investment
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Create a simple but credible value model for each priority use case, define the baseline, and specify what measurable success must look like before approving rollout.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Standardise pilot measurement and scale decision rules
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Build pilot scorecards, pre/post comparison logic, and explicit thresholds for scale, redesign, or stop decisions so evidence drives the next step.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Turn measurement into portfolio governance
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Use the existing KPI and ROI capability to review live AI performance on a regular cadence and make disciplined investment decisions across the portfolio.

actions.action_summary: Run an executive workshop that defines the AI thesis, top business problems, success outcomes, and sponsorship model.
actions.expected_outcome: Approved AI thesis with named sponsors
actions.action_detail:
